attributes.stem: An otherwise well 35-year old man presents to his general practitioner with a two-day history of hearing loss in his right ear. He has no tinnitus, vertigo, nausea, ear pain or discharge. Both tympanic membranes appear normal. Tuning fork tests will be performed to categorise the hearing loss. The short duration and otherwise normal examination suggest a right-sided sensorineural hearing loss.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which of the following tuning fork test results would suggest a right-sided sensorineural hearing loss?
options: Weber lateralising to the right ear, Rinne positive bilaterally
Weber lateralising to the right ear, Rinne negative on the right and positive on the left
Weber lateralising to the left ear, Rinne negative on the left and positive on the right
Weber lateralising to the left ear, Rinne positive bilaterally
attributes.explanation:
It is important to have an understanding of the underlying anatomy when considering hearing loss as it makes understanding the interpretation of the two common tests we use, the Weber Test and Rinne Test, easier to understand.

The cochlea receives sound via the air conduction pathway (ie. pinna, external ear canal, tympanic membrane, ossicles), but also directly by bone conduction (ie. directly via the skull, or from the skull then via ossicles and into the cochlea). The cochlea then processes sound energy into an electrical stimulus which is transmitted to the brain via the auditory nerve and pathways.

There are two types of hearing loss:

  • conductive hearing loss - abnormality transmitting sound from the outside world to the cochlea

  • sensorineural hearing loss - abnormality processing sound via the cochlea to the brain

The Weber Test

  • A vibrating tuning fork (ideally 512 Hz) is placed in the midline of the head. The patient is then asked where they hear the sound (eg. right side, left side, or the middle/no difference between ears).

  • Louder sound on one side is referred to as lateralisation.

    • If the test lateralises to the affected ear, conductive hearing loss is more likely (ie. hearing loss is reported in right ear, Weber test demonstrates lateralisation to the right)

    • If the test lateralises to the unaffected ear, sensorineural hearing loss is more likely (ie. hearing loss is reported in right ear, Weber test demonstrates lateralisation to the left)

    • No lateralisation can mean hearing is normal, or that symmetrical hearing loss is present

  • The Weber test should not be performed in isolation. It is important to always perform the Rinne test alongside the Weber test to properly screen for the likely cause of a hearing loss.

The Rinne Test

  • A vibrating tuning fork (ideally 512 Hz) on the patient's mastoid bone. The patient is asked to indicated when they can no longer hear the sound. The tuning fork is then held adjacent to the ear canal with the tines parallel to the canal. The patient is asked if they can hear the sound.

  • Rinne's test determines if air conduction is great or less than bone conduction in an individual ear.

    • A positive result means air conduction > bone conduction (ie. the tuning fork parallel to the ear was still heard once it was moved from the mastoid)

    • A negative result indicated that bone conduction > air conduction (ie. the tuning fork in front of the ear was not heard once it was moved from the mastoid).

  • The Rinne test should not be performed in isolation and Weber's test should also be used.

  • Sensitivity and specificity are limited for these screening tests which are fundamental components of the examination of a patient with hearing loss

  • Formal hearing testing via pure tone audiogram must be performed at the earliest opportunity

In this question, the patient has right sided hearing loss and that is likely due to a right-sided sensorineural defect. As such, on a Weber test lateralisation to the left side (the unaffected ear, as the cochlear in the unaffected ear is better functioning than that of the right) would be expected. On a Rinne test, the air conduction to be better than bone conduction bilaterally (ie. Rinne positive bilaterally). As such, Weber lateralising to the left ear, Rinne positive bilaterally is the most appropriate answer.

Weber lateralising to the right ear and Rinne positive bilaterally would suggest a left sensorineural defect or right conductive hearing loss.

Weber midline and Rinne positive bilaterally would suggest no hearing loss.

Weber lateralising to the right ear, Rinne negative on the right and positive on the left would suggest a right conductive hearing loss.

Weber lateralising to the left ear, Rinne negative on the left and positive on the right would suggest a left conductive hearing loss.
learningPoints: Lateralisation on a Weber test suggests ipsilateral conductive hearing loss or contralateral sensorineural loss
Don't perform a Weber or Rinne test in isolation
A Rinne test assesses air conduction - if it is negative then conductive hearing loss is suggested
references: Wahid NWB, Hogan CJ, Attia M. Weber Test. [Updated 2022 Feb 2]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2022 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK526135/
Kong EL, Fowler JB. Rinne Test. [Updated 2022 Feb 2]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2022 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK431071/
